title: Method for reducing oscillations in wind turbine blades
abstract: A system for a wind park including: a control system in communication with a plurality of unmanned air vehicles, wherein the control system is configured to deploy one or more unmanned air vehicles during a triggering condition; and wherein the deployed unmanned air vehicles are guided towards an assigned wind turbine and to interact with a blade of that wind turbine in order to control oscillation of the blade. The invention also embraces a method for reducing blade oscillations of a wind turbine, comprising: monitoring for a triggering condition associated with the wind turbine; on detecting the triggering condition, deploying unmanned air vehicles towards a wind turbine and interacting with a blade of the wind turbine using the unmanned air to control oscillation of the blade. The invention therefore provides an efficient approach to controlling blade oscillations with minimal human operator involvement. Drones may be deployed automatically once suitable conditions are detected and may automatically engage with the blades, either by contacting those blades physically, or by interacting with the blades in close proximity, in order to disrupt airflow around the blades thereby reducing oscillations.
claims: Claims 1. A system for a wind park including: a control system (31 ) in communication with a plurality of unmanned air vehicles (20); wherein the control system (31) is configured to deploy (56) the plurality of unmanned air vehicles during a triggering condition (54); and wherein the deployed unmanned air vehicles are guided towards an assigned wind turbine (58) and to interact with a blade (69) of that wind turbine in order to control oscillation of the blade. 2. The system of claim 1 , wherein the unmanned air vehicles are configured to apply a harness to a blade of the wind turbine. 3. The system of claim 2, wherein the unmanned air vehicles are configured to apply a force to the blade by pulling on the harness once the harness has been applied to the blade. 4. The system of claim 1 , wherein the unmanned air vehicles are configured to be attachable to the blade. 5. The system of claim 4, wherein the unmanned air vehicles include respective attachment means (80) for attaching said unmanned air vehicles to the blade. 6. The system of claim 5, wherein the attachment means comprises a suction cup device (80) for releasably attaching itself to the blade. 7. The system of claim 6, wherein the attachment means comprises a suction generator in order for the suction cup device to attach to a blade. 8. The system of claims 6 or 7, wherein the attachment means includes a suction release valve associated with the suction cup device to release suction from the suction cup device in order to detach the unmanned air vehicle from the surface of the blade. 9. The system of any one of claims 6 to 8, wherein the suction cup device is carried on an articulated arm (82). 10. The system of claim 5, wherein the attachment means includes a magnetic device (96) configured to couple to a magnetised portion of the blade. 1 1 . The system of claim 5, wherein the attachment means includes a gripper (98) configured to grip onto a portion of the blade. 12. The system of any one of claims 4 to 1 1 , wherein the unmanned air vehicles are configured to maintain predetermined relative positions from one another when attached to the blade. 13. The system of any one of claims 4 to 12, wherein the unmanned air vehicles are configured to change their mode of operation following attachment to the blade. 14. The system of claim 13, wherein the unmanned air vehicles switch to a reverse thrust mode of operation once attached to a respective blade. 15. The system of any one of claims 1 to 14, wherein the unmanned air vehicles comprise an autonomous flight control system (22) to guide said vehicles to the blade without human intervention. 16. The system of claim 15, wherein the unmanned air vehicle comprise a recognition system (100, 102) operable to recognise a wind turbine blade and guide the unmanned air vehicle into engagement with said blade. 17. The system of claim 16, wherein the recognition system comprises an optical camera system (102). 18. The system of any one of claims 1 to 17, comprising an operations base (66) for the unmanned air vehicles, wherein the unmanned air vehicles are stowed in the operation base when not on deployment. 19. The system of claim 18, wherein the operations base comprises a charging system for charging the unmanned air vehicles. 20. The system of claims 18 or 19, wherein the operations base comprises a door (70a, b) which is openable so as to enable deployment of the unmanned air vehicles from the operations base. 21 . A method for reducing blade oscillations of a wind turbine, comprising: Monitoring (54) for a triggering condition associated with the wind turbine; on detecting the triggering condition, deploying (56) unmanned air vehicles towards a wind turbine, interacting (62) with a blade of the wind turbine using the unmanned air vehicles to control oscillation of the blade. 22. The method of claim 21 , wherein interacting with the blade includes controlling the unmanned air vehicles to applying a harness to the blade using the unmanned air vehicles. 23. The method of claim 22, including pulling on the harness once the unmanned air vehicles have applied the harness to the blade. 24. The method of claim 21 , wherein interacting with the blade comprise commanding the unmanned air vehicles to fly in in close proximity to a respective blade so as to disrupt the airflow around the blade. 25. The method of claim 21 , wherein interacting with the blade includes attaching the unmanned air vehicles to the blade. 26. The method of claim 21 , wherein attaching said unmanned air vehicles to a respective blade comprises making surface contact with the blade and applying a force to the blade by way of that surface contact. 27. The method of claim 25, including, for each deployed unmanned air vehicle, activating an attachment means (80) to releasably attach said unmanned air vehicles to the blades. 28. The method of claim 27, further comprising activating an on-board suction generator on the unmanned air vehicle and bringing a suction cup of the unmanned air vehicle into engagement with the blade. 29. The method of claim 28, further including activating a suction release valve of the unmanned air vehicle to detach the suction cup from the blade. 30. The method of any one of claims 27 to 29, including changing a mode of operation of the unmanned air vehicles following attachment of said vehicles to the blade. 31 . The method of claim 30, wherein changing the mode of operation includes switching from a flight mode operation to a reverse thrust mode of operation. 32. A wind park comprising a plurality of wind turbines and a system as claimed in any one of claims 1 to 20.
